title.zh: 断首之后仍操干戚而舞的战神
title.en: The Headless God Who Still Dances with Shield and Axe

summary.zh: 与天帝争神位而被斩首的神，葬首常羊之山后以双乳为目、肚脐为口，一手持盾一手持斧，永不停歇地挥舞。
summary.en: A god beheaded for challenging the Supreme God's throne. With nipples for eyes and navel for mouth, he brandishes shield and axe in an endless war dance.

description.zh:
《海外西经》记："刑天与帝至此争神。帝断其首，葬之常羊之山。乃以乳为目，以脐为口，操干戚以舞。"干是盾，戚是斧。他与天帝（旧注多以为即黄帝）争夺神位，战败被斩首，头颅埋进常羊山。失去头颅的身躯没有倒下，两乳化为眼睛，肚脐化为嘴，双手仍握着盾与斧，向着看不见的敌人挥舞不止。

经文只有二十余字，却是《山海经》里最著名的败者形象之一。"刑"是戮；"天"一说指天帝，一说通"颠"、即头颅。这个名字本身就像战败后追加的刑名。东晋陶渊明《读山海经》组诗咏道"刑天舞干戚，猛志固常在"，把这具无首之躯读作至死不屈的意志，从此刑天与精卫填海并举，成为后世咏叹抗争的现成典故，也频繁出现在今天的小说、漫画与游戏里。
description.en:
The Classic of Regions Beyond the Seas: West devotes barely two dozen characters to him: Xingtian contended with the Supreme God for divinity; the god cut off his head and buried it on Mount Changyang; whereupon Xingtian made his nipples into eyes and his navel into a mouth, and danced brandishing shield and battle-axe. Early commentators often identify his opponent as the Yellow Emperor. The headless trunk never falls: it keeps swinging its weapons at an enemy it can no longer see.

The name itself reads like a sentence passed on the loser, roughly 'punished by Heaven' or 'the severed head'. The poet Tao Yuanming (365-427) fixed the figure's meaning for all later ages in his cycle 'On Reading the Shanhaijing': 'Xingtian dances with shield and axe; his fierce resolve endures forever.' Paired ever since with the sea-filling bird Jingwei as an emblem of defiance beyond defeat, Xingtian remains a stock figure in modern Chinese fiction, comics, and games.

value.en: Nipples for eyes, navel for mouth

sources.en: Shanhaijing, 'Classic of Regions Beyond the Seas: West'

sources.zh: 陶渊明《读山海经十三首》
sources.en: Tao Yuanming, 'On Reading the Shanhaijing', thirteen poems

volume.en: Classic of Regions Beyond the Seas: West
